citations.narrative:
There were four dog door inserts that had been excessively chewed by the bull dogs in those primary enclosures'. There
were deep grooves and tooth gouges located along all four sides of the framing inserts of the indoor portion of the doggy
doors.
The chewed material is not able to be sanitized due to the deep grooves in the plastic surface. The inability of being able
to sanitize a surface in contact with the regulated animals can become a possible health concern if the material is not
repaired or replaced.
All surfaces must be maintained on a regular basis. Surfaces of housing facilities - including houses, dens, and other
furniture-type fixtures and objects within the facility - that cannot be readily cleaned and sanitized, must be replaced when
worn or soiled.
To be corrected by: March 18, 2026

There were six outdoor runs of 16 enclosures that had not been spot cleaned daily. Six of the runs were not cleaned the
day before as the licensee ran out of time due to a prior commitment. At the time of inspection there were two days worth
of buildup in those six runs. The other 10 runs were within the daily pick up limit. There were four primary enclosures that
had a buildup of brown dirt/grime around the doors/door flap area that needed to be spot cleaned more often than the
current cleaning/sanitizing schedule of two weeks, in order to keep the primary enclosures at an acceptably clean
standard.
The lack of spot cleaning to provide clean runs on a daily basis, or increased spot cleaning of primary enclosure surfaces
when needed, can increase the risk of possible health issues with the regulated animals.
Hard surfaces with which the dogs or cats come in contact must be spot-cleaned daily and sanitized in accordance with §
3.11(b) of this subpart to prevent accumulation of excreta and reduce disease hazards. Floors made of dirt, absorbent
bedding, sand, gravel, grass, or other similar material must be raked or spot-cleaned with sufficient frequency to ensure
all animals the freedom to avoid contact with excreta. Contaminated material must be replaced whenever this raking and
spot-cleaning is not sufficient to prevent or eliminate odors, insects, pests, or vermin infestation. All other surfaces of
housing facilities must be cleaned and sanitized when necessary to satisfy generally accepted husbandry standards and
practices. Sanitization may be done using any of the methods provided in § 3.11(b)(3) for primary enclosures.
To be corrected by: March 15, 2026
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with licensee.
n

narrative:
3.1(c)(2) 
   
Housing facilities, general. 
There were four dog door inserts that had been excessively chewed by the bull dogs in those primary enclosures'. There 
were deep grooves and tooth gouges located along all four sides of the framing inserts of the indoor portion of the doggy 
doors. 
 
The chewed material is not able to be sanitized due to the deep grooves in the plastic surface. The inability of being able 
to sanitize a surface in contact with the regulated animals can become a possible health concern if the material is not 
repaired or replaced. 
 
All surfaces must be maintained on a regular basis. Surfaces of housing facilities - including houses, dens, and other 
furniture-type fixtures and objects within the facility - that cannot be readily cleaned and sanitized, must be replaced when 
worn or soiled. 
 
To be corrected by: March 18, 2026 
3.1(c)(3) 
   
Housing facilities, general. 
There were six outdoor runs of 16 enclosures that had not been spot cleaned daily. Six of the runs were not cleaned the 
day before as the licensee ran out of time due to a prior commitment. At the time of inspection there were two days worth 
of buildup in those six runs. The other 10 runs were within the daily pick up limit. There were four primary enclosures that 
had a buildup of brown dirt/grime around the doors/door flap area that needed to be spot cleaned more often than the 
current cleaning/sanitizing schedule of two weeks, in order to keep the primary enclosures at an acceptably clean 
standard. 
 
The lack of spot cleaning to provide clean runs on a daily basis, or increased spot cleaning of primary enclosure surfaces 
when needed, can increase the risk of possible health issues with the regulated animals. 
 
Hard surfaces with which the dogs or cats come in contact must be spot-cleaned daily and sanitized in accordance with § 
3.11(b) of this subpart to prevent accumulation of excreta and reduce disease hazards. Floors made of dirt, absorbent 
bedding, sand, gravel, grass, or other similar material must be raked or spot-cleaned with sufficient frequency to ensure

all animals the freedom to avoid contact with excreta. Contaminated material must be replaced whenever this raking and 
spot-cleaning is not sufficient to prevent or eliminate odors, insects, pests, or vermin infestation. All other surfaces of 
housing facilities must be cleaned and sanitized when necessary to satisfy generally accepted husbandry standards and 
practices. Sanitization may be done using any of the methods provided in § 3.11(b)(3) for primary enclosures. 
 
To be corrected by: March 15, 2026 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with licensee.
